evaluator.note: Benchmark v2. Blind subagent: packet-only context (prompt.md, diff-drift-report.md, raw-git-diff.patch); dispatched by the maintainer's agent session on 2026-06-10. Answer recorded verbatim.

findings.title: New dependency 'ghost-payments-sdk' added without lockfile update
findings.evidence: Raw diff adds "ghost-payments-sdk": "^1.0.0" to dependencies, but no lockfile change appears anywhere in the patch. The package was never installed in this change, so its existence and provenance are unverified — consistent with a hallucinated or typo/slopsquat-prone package name, which is a known agent failure mode. For a checkout/payments service this is exactly the supply-chain vector (fake payment SDK exfiltrating card data) that must be confirmed on the registry before merge.

findings.title: New postinstall hook executes a script not included in the change
findings.evidence: Diff adds "postinstall": "node scripts/bootstrap.js", which runs automatically on every npm install for every developer and CI machine. The raw diff contains no scripts/bootstrap.js, so the code it executes is either pre-existing and unreviewed in this context or missing entirely (install would fail). An agent-introduced install-time execution hook paired with an unverified new dependency is a classic supply-chain pattern and cannot be trusted without inspecting bootstrap.js.

notes: Diff Drift's two flags match the raw diff exactly; no false positives and no changes were missed (the diff touches only package.json).
Diff Drift rated the postinstall flag medium severity; given the missing/unreviewed scripts/bootstrap.js target and the payments context, high seems more appropriate, but the flag itself is accurate.
If review shows ghost-payments-sdk is a legitimate internal/registry package, the lockfile should still be regenerated and bootstrap.js reviewed before approval.
